# The Ultimate Systems Programming Academy — full page index

> Every English page of the Academy, grouped by section. The Português (pt-BR)
> mirror of each page lives under the /pt/ path. See llms.txt for the summary.

## Home
- [Systems Programming Academy](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/): Learn production software engineering from absolute beginner to advanced with Python, Go, and Rust.

## Start Here
- [About the Author](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/about-the-author/): Walter Angolar — systems engineer and builder from Angola. Creator of the Delonix container engine, the Ayize programming language, and this academy.
- [How to use this Academy](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/how-to-use/): Features of the platform — search, progress tracking, dark mode, running the code, and offline use.
- [Welcome](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/welcome/): What this Academy is, who it is for, and the standards every lesson is held to.

## Toolchains & Contributing
- [The Toolchains: cargo · uv · go](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/toolchains/cli-tools/): The command-line tools every example in this academy uses — how to build, run, test, format, lint, and ship releases in Rust (cargo), Python (uv), and Go, plus the config files (Cargo.toml, pyproject.toml, go.mod) that hold it all together.
- [Where to Contribute: Open-Source Projects](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/toolchains/contributing/): Curated, beginner-friendly open-source projects in Rust, Go, and Python where you can put this academy's knowledge to work — how to find good first issues, the etiquette that gets a PR merged, and a starting list for each language.
- [Git & GitHub for Contributors](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/toolchains/git-github/): A practical guide to the version-control workflow that open source runs on — the core git commands (init, clone, branch, commit, pull, push, merge, rebase, cherry-pick, worktree, stash) with the collaborative context of when to reach for each, plus the GitHub CLI (gh) that operates GitHub from your terminal.

## Part 1 · Foundations
- [Go: Slices, Maps & Strings](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/go/collections/): The three workhorse types in Go — slices as views over a backing array, maps as hash tables, and strings as immutable UTF-8. Their idioms and sharp edges, with complete tested examples.
- [Go: Goroutines & Channels](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/go/concurrency/): Go's signature feature — lightweight goroutines and typed channels for communicating between them. Buffered vs unbuffered, select, sync.WaitGroup and Mutex, and the race detector, with deterministic tested examples.
- [Go: Error Handling](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/go/error-handling/): Errors are values in Go — returned, checked, and wrapped, not thrown. The error interface, sentinel errors, custom error types, %w wrapping, and errors.Is/As, with complete tested examples.
- [Go: Generics](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/go/generics/): Type parameters (Go 1.18+) let one function or type work for many element types with compile-time safety. Constraints, the ~ operator, comparable and any, and generic types — with complete tested examples.
- [Go: Structs, Methods & Interfaces](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/go/interfaces/): Go's type system in one lesson — structs, value vs pointer receivers, implicitly-satisfied interfaces, and composition by embedding. The Go way to model behaviour, with complete tested examples.
- [Go: Testing & Documentation](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/go/testing/): Go's testing tool is built in — no framework needed. Table-driven tests, subtests, runnable examples that double as docs, and benchmarks, all with a tested companion module.
- [Python: The Data Model](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/python/data-model/): Dunder methods are how your objects plug into Python's syntax — len(), indexing, iteration, arithmetic, repr. Implement __len__/__getitem__/__repr__/__eq__/__add__ and the language works for your types too. Complete tested examples.
- [Python: Iterators & Generators](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/python/iterators/): The iterator protocol powers every for loop, and generators build lazy iterators with yield. Infinite sequences, generator pipelines, and streaming huge data with tiny memory — with complete tested examples.
- [Python: Protocols & Duck Typing](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/python/protocols/): If it quacks like a duck, it's a duck. Python's duck typing made checkable with typing.Protocol — structural typing with no inheritance, plus @runtime_checkable and how Protocols compare to ABCs. Complete tested examples.
- [Python: Testing](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/python/testing/): Testing with the stdlib unittest and pytest — fixtures, exception assertions, subtests/parametrization, mocking with unittest.mock, and doctests. A fully tested bank-account module.
- [Python: Type Hints](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/python/type-hints/): Python is dynamic, but type hints let tools catch bugs before you run. Annotations, Optional/Union with the | syntax, generics, and dataclasses — with a tested module and the mutable-default trap explained.
- [Rust: Collections & Iterators](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/rust/collections/): Vec, HashMap and String, and the lazy iterator adapters that make them ergonomic and zero-cost — with complete, tested examples and a benchmark proving iterator chains rival hand-written loops.
- [Rust: Concurrency Basics](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/rust/concurrency/): Fearless concurrency — how Rust's ownership rules stop data races at compile time. Threads, move closures, channels, and shared state with Arc<Mutex>, all with deterministic, tested examples.
- [Rust: Error Handling with Result, Option & ?](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/rust/error-handling/): How Rust turns errors into ordinary values you cannot ignore — Result, Option, the ? operator, custom error types, and when to panic — with complete, runnable examples.
- [Rust: Generics & Lifetimes](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/rust/generics/): Write code once that works for many types, and let the compiler prove your references are always valid. Generic functions and structs, trait bounds, and lifetime annotations — with complete, tested examples.
- [Rust: Ownership & the Borrow Checker](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/rust/ownership/): The one idea that makes Rust different — how ownership, moves, borrowing and lifetimes give you memory safety with no garbage collector, explained and measured.
- [Rust: Smart Pointers & Interior Mutability](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/rust/smart-pointers/): Box, Rc and RefCell — heap allocation, shared ownership, and mutation through a shared reference. When you need them, how they compose as Rc<RefCell<T>>, and the cycle trap — with tested examples.
- [Rust: Testing & Documentation](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/rust/testing/): Rust ships a test runner and a doc-test system in the box. Learn unit tests, integration tests, doc-tests that keep examples honest, and should_panic — with a fully tested companion crate.
- [Rust: Types & Traits](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/rust/traits/): Traits are how Rust describes shared behaviour — interfaces resolved either at compile time (generics) or run time (dyn). Learn required vs default methods, static vs dynamic dispatch, impl Trait and object safety, with complete tested examples.
- [Rust: Unsafe & FFI](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-1-foundations/rust/unsafe-ffi/): What `unsafe` really unlocks (five abilities, not chaos), how to wrap an unsafe core in a safe API, raw pointers, and calling C both ways via FFI — with a tested reimplementation of split_at_mut.

## Part 2 · Real-World Engineering
- [Caching](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-2-engineering/caching/): A TTL cache with an injected clock (no sleeps in tests), hit/miss counters, and the cache-aside pattern proven by a call-counting backend. The same design in Rust, Go, and Python — plus invalidation, stampedes, and when not to cache.
- [CI/CD](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-2-engineering/ci-cd/): Continuous integration and delivery, dissected through a real pipeline — this Academy's own ci.yml, which fmt/lint/tests 38 modules across Rust, Go, and Python matrices and builds the site on every push.
- [Configuration & Secrets](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-2-engineering/configuration/): Parse the environment into typed config once, fail fast, inject the source for testability, and redact secrets so logs can't leak them. The same design in Rust, Go, and Python — with tested modules in all three.
- [Clean & Hexagonal Architecture](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-2-engineering/hexagonal-architecture/): Domain, use cases, adapters, composition root — the Dependency Rule made concrete with one task tracker layered identically in Rust, Go, and Python, including an executable architecture test that fails if a layer imports outward.
- [Logging & Observability](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-2-engineering/logging/): Structured, levelled, testable logging — key-value JSON lines, an injected sink, and request-scoped context fields. The same design in Rust, Go (log/slog), and Python (logging) — with tested modules in all three.
- [Repository Pattern & Dependency Injection](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-2-engineering/repository-pattern/): Decouple business logic from storage with a port-and-adapter design, and inject the dependency so you can test without a database. The same design in Rust, Go, and Python, side by side — with tested modules in all three.
- [The Testing Pyramid](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-2-engineering/testing-pyramid/): Many fast unit tests, fewer integration tests, one end-to-end test — the same signup feature tested at all three levels in Rust, Go, and Python, showing what each level catches and costs.

## Part 3 · DevOps Automation
- [Project: Config Generator](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-3-devops/config-generator/): Generate nginx and systemd configs from a validated service spec, in Rust, Go, and Python — byte-identical output, golden-file tests, and a why-X analysis where maintainability, not speed, decides.
- [Project: Health-Check Agent](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-3-devops/health-check/): Probe a fleet of TCP targets in parallel with connect timeouts — scoped threads, goroutines, and a thread pool doing real ops work. Deterministic reports, scripting-friendly exit codes, and tests against real sockets.
- [Project: Log Analyzer CLI](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-3-devops/log-analyzer/): Build the same access-log analyzer in Rust, Go, and Python — tested, byte-identical output — then benchmark them for real - 98ms vs 131ms vs 736ms on 500k lines - and weigh when each language earns the job.
- [Project: Prometheus-Style Exporter](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-3-devops/prometheus-exporter/): Build a metrics exporter serving the Prometheus text format over HTTP in Rust, Go, and Python — live-verified byte-identical — and weigh the long-running-agent trade-offs - resident memory 2.0 vs 7.6 vs 21.3 MB.

## Part 4 · Concurrency & Parallelism
- [Async & Event-Driven I/O](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-4-concurrency/async-io/): One thread, ten thousand waits — a poll(2) event loop built by hand in Rust, Go's invisible netpoller, and Python's asyncio, with the 10k-concurrent-waits benchmark that explains why async exists.
- [Message Passing & Channels](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-4-concurrency/message-passing/): Instead of locking shared state, transfer ownership of values between threads — a worker pool built on Go channels, Rust mpsc, and Python queue.Queue, with 1M-message throughput measured for every buffering flavour.
- [Project: mini-NGINX](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-4-concurrency/mini-nginx/): Build a concurrent static-file HTTP server three ways — Rust thread pool, Go goroutine-per-connection, Python asyncio — with no web framework. Byte-identical responses, path-traversal defence, and a real load test at 20k requests.
- [Threads & Shared State](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-4-concurrency/threads-shared-state/): One counter, four threads, four million increments — measured with a data race, a mutex, an atomic, and batching in Rust, Go, and Python. Where the compiler saves you, where the race detector does, and where the GIL changes the game.

## Part 5 · Building Frameworks
- [DI Container: Wiring the Object Graph](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-5-frameworks/di-container/): Build a dependency-injection container in Rust, Go, and Python — registration, singleton vs transient lifetimes, and cycle detection that turns a stack overflow into a clear error. The same eight tests pass in all three.
- [Query Builder: Fluent APIs & SQL Injection](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-5-frameworks/query-builder/): Build a chainable SQL query builder in Rust, Go, and Python. The builder pattern turns method calls into SQL — and parameterized placeholders make injection structurally impossible. Byte-identical SQL verified across all three.
- [Routing & Middleware: Build a Web Framework](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-5-frameworks/routing-middleware/): The step from library to framework is inversion of control. Build a micro web framework — routing table, path parameters, and a composable middleware chain — in Rust, Go, and Python, with the same nine tests passing in all three.
- [Serialization: A JSON Encoder & Parser](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-5-frameworks/serialization/): Build a JSON serialization framework in Rust, Go, and Python — a canonical encoder and a recursive-descent parser, from scratch. Escaping for the JSON grammar (not HTML), deterministic byte-identical output, and a round trip proven across all three.
- [Template Engine: Autoescaping & XSS](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-5-frameworks/template-engine/): Build a template engine in Rust, Go, and Python with HTML autoescaping by default — the output-side twin of the query builder's injection defence. A composable filter pipeline, an explicit raw opt-out, and byte-identical rendered HTML across all three.
- [Test Framework: Building the Tool We Used](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-5-frameworks/test-framework/): Build a tiny test framework in Rust, Go, and Python — assertions, a runner that isolates failures and catches panics, and the canonical pass/fail report you read after every lesson. Byte-identical output across all three.
- [Validation Framework: Declare, Don't Check](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-5-frameworks/validation/): Build a declarative validation framework in Rust, Go, and Python. Declare a schema of rules per field; the evaluator accumulates every error at once instead of failing fast. Byte-identical error output across all three.

## Part 6 · Command-Line Tools & Dashboards
- [Project: eBPF Observability — A Latency Histogram](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-6-cli-tools/ebpf-observability/): Build the userspace half of an eBPF latency tool in Rust, Go, and Python — the log2 bucketing and the classic power-of-two histogram, tested and byte-identical. The kernel-side eBPF program that feeds it, and the bpf() syscall that loads it, are shown in full and explained.
- [Capstone: netdiag — One CLI Over the Five Tools](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-6-cli-tools/netdiag/): The Part 6 capstone: unify the performance dashboard, port scanner, ping, and traceroute under one command with subcommand dispatch and a shared, elegant report format — the command layer tested and byte-identical across Rust, Go, and Python.
- [Project: A Performance Dashboard (CPU · MEM · I/O · Net)](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-6-cli-tools/performance-dashboard/): Build a live terminal dashboard that reads Linux /proc and renders CPU, memory, disk, and network as colored heat bars — in Rust, Go, and Python. Pure parsing and ANSI rendering, tested with fixtures, byte-identical frames across all three, and a real live monitor.
- [Project: ping — ICMP Echo & Round-Trip Time](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-6-cli-tools/ping/): Build ping in Rust, Go, and Python — the ICMP echo packet, the RFC 1071 internet checksum, reply parsing, and round-trip statistics, all tested and byte-identical. The raw socket that sends it is the one piece that needs root, shown in full and handled gracefully.
- [Project: A Concurrent Port Scanner (nmap-lite)](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-6-cli-tools/port-scanner/): Build a concurrent TCP connect scanner in Rust, Go, and Python — parse a port spec, probe hundreds of ports in parallel, and print an elegant results table. Tested against real loopback sockets, byte-identical output, no privileges.
- [Part 6 in Review — From /proc to the Kernel](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-6-cli-tools/synthesis/): A reflective tour of Part 6: how six command-line tools trace a single throughline from userspace to the kernel, the recurring discipline of testing the logic and isolating the privilege, and what each tool taught.
- [Project: traceroute — Mapping the Path, Hop by Hop](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/part-6-cli-tools/traceroute/): Build traceroute in Rust, Go, and Python by stepping the IP TTL and reading the ICMP time-exceeded replies each router sends back. The probe, checksum, ICMP classifier, and report are pure and byte-identical; the raw socket that sets the TTL is the one privileged piece.

## Capstone & Synthesis
- [Capstone: A Secure Guestbook, End to End](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/capstone/guestbook/): The whole academy in one small app. Compose validation, parameterized queries, and autoescaped templating into a guestbook, then attack it with a SQL-injection and an XSS payload — and watch both defences hold. Built and defeated in Rust, Go, and Python.
- [Running It for Real: HTTP + SQLite](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/capstone/running-app/): Promote the in-memory capstone to a real running web app — a concurrent HTTP server backed by a real SQLite database, in Rust, Go, and Python. The SQL-injection proof now runs against an actual SQL engine, and three live servers render byte-identical pages.
- [The Three Languages: A Measured Comparison](https://angolardevops.github.io/systems-programming-academy/capstone/three-languages/): Ninety pages of teaching Python, Go, and Rust against each other produced real numbers — startup times, memory, throughput, the cost of a lock. The synthesis of what the measurements actually say, and how to choose, grounded entirely in the academy's own data.
